mainmatter.p: Let R \colon A \mathbin { \nrightarrow } B be a relation. The direct image of X \subseteq A under R is the set R _* X \subseteq B defined below:
mainmatter.tex: R _* X = { \mathopen {} \left \{ b \in B \, \middle \vert \, \exists x \in X \mathpunct {.} x \mathrel {R}b \right \} \mathclose {}}

mainmatter.p: Let R \colon A \mathbin { \nrightarrow } B be a relation. The inverse image of Y \subseteq B under R is the set R ^* Y \subseteq A defined below:

A (binary) relation R from a set A to a set B, written R \colon A \mathbin { \nrightarrow } B or R \in \mathrm {Rel} { \mathopen {} \left ( A,B \right ) \mathclose {}} is defined to be a subset R \subseteq A \times B. We shall typically write a \mathrel {R}b for { \mathopen {} \left ( a,b \right ) \mathclose {}} \in R. More generally, a relation between multiple sets { \mathopen {} \left ( A_i \right ) \mathclose {}} _{i \in I} is defined to be a subset of the cartesian product \prod _{i \in I} A_i.

Let I be a set, and let \mathtt {P} { \mathopen {} \left ( \mathtt {x}, \mathtt {y} \right ) \mathclose {}} be a formula in the language of set theory such that \forall i \in I \mathpunct {.} \exists ! S \mathpunct {.} \mathtt {P} { \mathopen {} \left ( i,S \right ) \mathclose {}} holds. Then the collection of sets S such that \mathtt {P} { \mathopen {} \left ( i,S \right ) \mathclose {}} holds for some i \in I holds forms a set.

This is almost saying that the direct image of I under \mathtt {P} is a set, but this doesn’t actually make sense because we do not have a way to speak about a “relation” between a set and the class of all sets. This strange situation is clarified by other foundational system that go beyond the limitations of the Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory that we have studied in this course.
